title: Redis
objectives: Use Redis for caching, sessions and rate limiting

sections.markdown: Redis is an in-memory data store with rich types (strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, streams). Sub-millisecond reads make it the default cache + rate-limit + session store, and its atomic ops enable clever patterns.

Cache patterns to know: **cache-aside** (app loads DB on miss, writes to cache), **write-through** (write DB + cache together), **write-behind** (write cache, sync to DB later). Cache-aside is the default; watch for stampedes on hot keys (mitigate with request coalescing / TTL jitter).

Using SETNX for distributed locking without a token + release check (someone else's expired lock gets released by you). Use the Redlock pattern for multi-node; for single-node use SET NX PX + a fencing token.
Choose an eviction policy (allkeys-lru for pure caches; volatile-ttl mixed). Set maxmemory. Persist with RDB snapshots + AOF for durability if used as a DB. Never store secrets in Redis in cleartext.

flashcards.back: Cache-aside: read from cache; on miss load DB, write to cache with TTL.

flashcards.front: What is a sorted set used for?
flashcards.back: Leaderboards, priority queues, rate-limit windows — score-ordered members with O(log N) ops.
SET lock:key <token> NX PX <ms> — set only if absent, with an expiry.

options: App loads DB on miss then caches
quiz.explain: Application controls the cache; DB is source of truth.
Sorted sets keep members ordered by score.

challenges.text: Implement cache-aside for a hot GET endpoint with TTL.
Build a token-bucket rate limiter with a Lua script.
Build a leaderboard: add score, top-K, user rank via sorted sets.
Implement a Redlock-style distributed lock for a critical job.

interviewQuestions: Which cache pattern do you use and why?
How do you prevent cache stampedes?
How would you build a rate limiter with Redis?
Trade-offs of using Redis as a primary DB?
